patterns.what_to_look_for: Endpoints that resolve the tenant/org/workspace/account id from request-controlled input (X-Tenant-Id header, body/query field, path segment, or an unverified JWT claim) and feed it straight into queries, instead of deriving tenant from the authenticated session and checking membership.
source_indicators: req.headers['x-tenant-id'] / request.headers.get('X-Org-Id') used in a query
tenantId = req.body.tenantId or params[:account_id] passed to .where(tenant_id: ...)
JWT 'tenant'/'org' claim decoded and trusted without re-checking user membership
WHERE tenant_id = $tenantFromRequest with no membership join
tenant id read from a client cookie/localStorage and echoed into the data query
blackbox_indicators: Changing X-Tenant-Id / org_id body field returns another tenant's data with 200
Tenant id embedded in JWT or cookie is editable and still honored
Object ids are global and swapping tenant context yields 200 instead of 403
evidence_required: Two-account proof: account A reads account B's record by altering the tenant identifier
Request/response pair showing the client-supplied tenant id is honored
Confirmation the id is never cross-checked against session membership
false_positive_checks: Send a mismatched tenant id with A's session: a 403 means server re-derives from session
A scoped query / policy / RLS actually filters by the session tenant
Returned record belongs to the caller's own tenant (same id, no cross-access)
patterns.suggested_validation_task: With two test tenants, send tenant A's session but tenant B's id in the header/body on a read-only GET and check whether B's record is returned.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as cross-tenant broken object-level authorization (IDOR); attach the two-account read and the unenforced client-supplied tenant id.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Data-access paths (raw SQL, ORM find-by-primary-key, joins, reports/exports, admin tools) that omit the tenant_id predicate and rely on a global scope or middleware that does not cover every path; or Postgres RLS that is disabled or bypassed because the app connects as the table owner / a BYPASSRLS role.
source_indicators: Model.findByPk(id) / Model.objects.get(pk=id) with no tenant filter
raw query SELECT * FROM invoices WHERE id = ? with no tenant_id
global/default scope defined but .unscoped / withoutGlobalScope / .raw() used on this path
RLS policy exists but ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, or app role is table owner / has BYPASSRLS
new endpoint or background path that does not pass through tenant-scoping middleware
blackbox_indicators: Sequential/guessable primary keys return records regardless of caller's tenant
Endpoint returns objects whose tenant differs from the authenticated caller
Search/report/export endpoint includes rows from other tenants
evidence_required: A record fetched by id that belongs to a different tenant than the authenticated caller
Query trace/log showing no tenant predicate on the executed statement
For RLS: proof the app's actual DB role bypasses or has RLS disabled
false_positive_checks: A default/global scope is applied and provably covers this exact path
DB enforces RLS using the app's real (non-owner, non-superuser) role
All returned ids fall within the caller's tenant
patterns.suggested_validation_task: As tenant A, request a known tenant-B object id through the endpoint (read-only) and confirm whether the record is returned.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as broken tenant isolation at the data layer; cite the unscoped query/path (or RLS bypass) and the cross-tenant read.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Cache keys (Redis/Memcached, in-process memo, HTTP/CDN edge cache) constructed without a tenant component, so one tenant's cached entry or response is served to another; or tenant-specific HTTP responses marked publicly cacheable without Vary on the tenant discriminator.
source_indicators: cache.set('user:' + id, ...) or `profile:${userId}` keyed by a global id, no tenant namespace
process-level singleton/memo populated by the first tenant's request and reused
Cache-Control: public on tenant-specific responses with no Vary on tenant cookie/header
CDN/edge cache keyed on path only while the tenant lives in a header/cookie
shared cache namespace across tenants (no tenant prefix in key builder)
blackbox_indicators: Priming an endpoint as tenant A then requesting it as tenant B returns A's data with a cache HIT
Same URL returns different tenants' content depending on which warmed the cache
Cache-Control: public / non-zero Age headers on personalized or tenant-scoped data
evidence_required: Demonstration that tenant B receives tenant A's cached response (X-Cache HIT)
Cache key construction showing no tenant scoping
Response headers permitting shared caching of tenant-specific data
false_positive_checks: Full cache key/namespace includes the tenant id (inspect the actual key, not the prefix)
Vary or private/no-store directives are present and honored by CDN/proxy
Cached data is non-tenant public/reference data
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Prime an endpoint as tenant A, then request the same path as tenant B (read-only) and check for a cache HIT serving A's data.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as cross-tenant data leakage via shared cache; show the un-namespaced key or cacheable tenant response and the cross-served HIT.

patterns.what_to_look_for: A single signing/encryption secret shared across all tenants combined with a tenant id carried inside the token/signed artifact, so a token minted for tenant A is accepted for tenant B; or predictable/sequential tenant ids; or per-tenant API keys validated without binding the key to the resource's tenant.
source_indicators: JWT signed with one global secret and the tenant_id claim trusted without re-binding to the principal
API-key lookup returns the key row but never compares its tenant to the requested resource's tenant
tenant ids are auto-increment integers / short sequential values
shared HMAC secret used for signed URLs across all tenants
encryption/data key not derived per tenant (same KMS key for every tenant)
blackbox_indicators: Editing the tenant id inside a JWT or signed URL and reusing the same signature is accepted
Tenant ids appearing in URLs are small sequential integers
An API key issued to tenant A authorizes actions against tenant B resources
evidence_required: A token/signed artifact issued for one tenant being accepted for another
Proof the tenant id is predictable or enumerable
Showing the auth check validates the key/signature but not the tenant binding
false_positive_checks: Token tenant claim is re-validated against the authenticated principal's membership
Per-tenant keys are actually in use (a cross-tenant signature fails verification)
Tenant ids are random UUIDs and the binding is enforced server-side
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Replay a request with another tenant's id substituted into the token/signed URL (signature unchanged) and observe accept vs reject (read-only).
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as cross-tenant authentication bypass via shared/forgeable keys; include the cross-tenant token acceptance.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Tenant resolved from the Host header / subdomain / a routing header and used to select the database, schema, connection, or data partition, trusting a spoofable header as the security boundary, with no cross-check that the authenticated user belongs to the host-resolved tenant.
source_indicators: tenant = req.hostname.split('.')[0] used to pick a schema/connection
SET search_path = $subdomain or USE `${tenant}_db` driven by the host
connection pool / datasource keyed by the Host header value
tenant taken from X-Forwarded-Host or another proxy header and trusted
no check that the session's tenant equals the host-resolved tenant
blackbox_indicators: Overriding Host / X-Forwarded-Host changes which tenant's data is returned
Changing the subdomain accesses another tenant while the same session token still works
Errors leak schema/db names that match tenant slugs
evidence_required: A request with a spoofed Host/subdomain returning a different tenant's data
Source/trace showing the partition is selected from the host/header
Confirmation that a session-tenant != host-tenant mismatch is not rejected
false_positive_checks: Host is validated against an allowlist and matched to the session tenant
Routing tenant is cross-checked with the auth tenant (mismatch returns 403)
Partition selection ignores the client host and uses the session tenant
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Send your authenticated session with another tenant's subdomain/Host header on a read-only GET and check whether that tenant's data is served.
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as tenant isolation bypass via untrusted host-based partition routing; show the spoofed-Host cross-tenant read.

patterns.what_to_look_for: Background jobs, queue consumers, scheduled tasks, bulk exports, or webhook handlers that do not carry/restore tenant context and fall back to a default/global connection or a stale request-scoped tenant, causing cross-tenant reads or writes in the async path.
source_indicators: job enqueued without a tenant_id; the worker runs under the default scope/connection
request-scoped thread-local / async-local tenant not propagated into the job or promise
Current.tenant (request-scoped) read inside a worker where it is nil or stale
export/report builder iterates rows with no tenant filter
webhook handler resolves tenant from the payload without verifying signature/ownership
blackbox_indicators: An exported file/report contains rows belonging to other tenants
An async-generated artifact (PDF/email) references another tenant's data
Replaying a webhook with an altered tenant id processes it for the wrong tenant
evidence_required: Code/trace showing tenant context is missing or defaulted in the async path
Repro tying the async trigger to the leaked tenant
false_positive_checks: tenant_id is serialized into the job payload and re-scoped in the worker
Webhook tenant is verified via signature plus ownership check
patterns.suggested_validation_task: Trigger a benign export/report as tenant A and inspect whether any returned rows belong to other tenants (read-only inspection).
patterns.reporting_guidance: Report as tenant isolation failure in async/background processing; include the cross-tenant rows present in the generated artifact.
